steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is a Boundaries question. The blank falls between a main clause ending in "previously _____" and the noun phrase "a finding that, if true, would overturn current theories..." That noun phrase is a nonrestrictive appositive elaborating on the idea of the earlier-than-expected date. The question is which punctuation mark correctly attaches the appositive to the main clause.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — "supposed a": No punctuation between the main clause and the appositive creates a run-on — the reader cannot tell where the clause ends and the appositive begins. ✗
  • Choice B — "supposed. A": A period turns "a finding that..." into a sentence fragment; a noun phrase alone cannot stand as a complete sentence. ✗
  • Choice C — "supposed, a": A comma correctly sets off the appositive noun phrase from the preceding main clause, following standard convention for appositives. ✓
  • Choice D — "supposed; a": A semicolon must be followed by an independent clause. "A finding that, if true, would overturn current theories..." is a noun phrase, not an independent clause, so the semicolon is incorrect here. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
